summary: Jeff Atwood examines the problem of uncivil discourse in online comments, using a rude comment on Wil Shipley's blog as a springboard. He argues that while criticism is valuable, resorting to personal attacks and angry invective is self-defeating because it causes people to stop listening. Drawing on Miss Manners' wisdom about etiquette and Martin Luther King Jr.'s Letter From Birmingham Jail as a model of effective disagreement, Atwood concludes that the only way to persuade others is through civil, reasonable, and rational discourse. The post acknowledges the 'Greater Internet Fuckwad Theory' as a byproduct of online anonymity but insists we can do better.
key_points: Angry personal attacks negate any legitimate criticism you're trying to make - the minute you insult someone, they stop listening
Online anonymity enables bad behavior (the G.I.F.T.), but this isn't an excuse for incivility
Etiquette has social sanctions: people who behave badly get isolated and excluded from communities
Martin Luther King Jr.'s Letter From Birmingham Jail demonstrates how to express profound disagreement while maintaining respect
Effective persuasion requires respecting those you disagree with and earning their respect in turn
money_quotes: The minute you call someone an a**hole, they're no longer listening to you.
If you behave in a way that offends the people you're trying to deal with, they will stop dealing with you.
Insulting someone might make you feel temporarily vindicated, or give you a brief, cathartic moment of release, but you aren't convincing anyone of anything.
The fine art of disagreement: the ability to respect the people you disagree with, and to earn their respect in turn.
The only way to do that is to be civil, reasonable, and rational.
I don't object to criticism. Criticism is what comments are for. What I object to is criticism that resorts to least-common-denominator attacks.
The use of angry invective negates any criticism you were trying to make.
key_insight: Effective disagreement requires civility - angry attacks are self-defeating because they cause your audience to dismiss both your argument and you.
